perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to any and all Information of a confidential, proprietary, or secret nature which is applicable to or related In any way to (i) the business, present or future, of the Disclosing Party, (ii) the research and development or investigations of the Disclosing Party or (iii) the business of any customer of the Disclosing Party; provided , in each case , that such information is delivered to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party and (a) is marked or identified in writing as “Confidential”, (b) if verbal or visual disclosure, is identified as “Confidential” in a writing within ten (10) business days of such disclosure, or (c) is information that the Receiving Party actually knew or reasonably should have known was confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to Information that the Disclosing Party explicitly designates as ‘Proprietary Information’ at the time of disclosure, and only includes trade secrets and patented technologies. No other information, regardless of its sensitivity or competitive value, shall be considered Proprietary Information under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change narrows the definition of Proprietary Information in Section 1 to only include information explicitly designated as such, limiting it to trade secrets and patented technologies. This contradicts the broader description of Proprietary Information provided later in the same section and also the later use of the phrase, creating an internal contradiction. It creates uncertainty about what information is protected under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used herein, the term “Proprietary Information” refers to Information that the Disclosing Party explicitly designates as ‘Proprietary Information’ at the time of disclosure, and only includes trade secrets and patented technologies. No other information, regardless of its sensitivity or competitive value, shall be considered Proprietary Information under this Agreement. Proprietary Information may include, for example and without limitation, the product offering, content partners, product pricing, product availability, computer programs, technical drawings, algorithms, processes, ideas, schematics, trade secrets, processes, formulas, data, know-how, improvements, inventions (whether patentable or not), techniques, marketing plans, forecasts and strategies, and information concerning customers or vendors. Proprietary Information shall also include all information of a like nature owned by any other person and furnished to the Disclosing Party by such other person pursuant to an undertaking by the Disclosing Party to maintain the same in confidence.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of "Proprietary Information" to only include explicitly designated information, trade secrets, and patented technologies. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text', which provides a much broader, non-exclusive list of examples of what Proprietary Information may include, like product offerings, pricing, marketing plans, customer information, etc. This introduces a strong in-text contradiction because the changed definition limits what can be considered Proprietary Information, while the later text assumes a much wider scope, creating uncertainty and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information with the same level of care as it holds its own non-sensitive information, (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, unless the Receiving Party deems such disclosure necessary for its own legitimate business purposes, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, or for any other purpose the Receiving Party deems beneficial, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information unless required for compliance with local laws.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the Receiving Party's obligations regarding Proprietary Information in Section 2. Original text demands strict confidentiality and no use of information, but the change allows disclosure if 'necessary for its own legitimate business purposes' and usage if 'Receiving Party deems beneficial'. This creates a contradiction with the original intent and creates uncertainty on the enforcement of the restrictions, particularly as other sections rely on a strong understanding of strict non-disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 2 significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligations regarding Proprietary Information, allowing for disclosure if deemed 'necessary for its own legitimate business purposes' and usage if the 'Receiving Party deems beneficial.' This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states that 'there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement' and that such a breach may cause 'irreparable harm.' The relaxation of confidentiality requirements in the changed text directly conflicts with the strict confidentiality implied in the contradicted text, making it easier for a breach to occur and harder to enforce the agreement's remedies. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party at any time (which will be effective if actually received or three days after mailed first class postage prepaid to the Receiving Party’s address herein), the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party, or, at its sole discretion, destroy and certify to the Receiving party in writing the destruction of all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof; provided, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the expiration of this Agreement, the Receiving Party, at its sole discretion, may either return to the Disclosing Party or destroy all Proprietary Information, but is under no obligation to do either. There is no requirement to certify destruction, and any retained information remains subject to the confidentiality obligations outlined in this Agreement, however, the Company and its Representatives shall not be obligated to return or destroy Proprietary Information to the extent otherwise required by law, regulation, rule or practice governing professionals or any internal compliance policy or procedure required by law, regulation rule of practice governing professionals.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the obligation to return or destroy Proprietary Information in Section 3. Originally, upon request, the Receiving Party *will* return or destroy. The change grants sole discretion to the Receiving Party with no obligation, contradicting any implied right of the Disclosing Party to reclaim their information and the understanding of Proprietary Information being sensitive in the definition from Section 1, specifically, creating an in-text contradiction. It also removes the certification requirement, reducing accountability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence and to take all reasonable precautions to protect such Proprietary Information (including, without limitation, all precautions that Receiving Party employs with respect to its confidential materials), (b) not to divulge any such Proprietary Information or any information derived therefrom to any third person without prior written permission of the Disclosing Party, excluding disclosure to Representatives, (c) not to make any use whatsoever at any time of such Proprietary Information except to evaluate internally whether to enter into the contemplated business relationship with the Disclosing Party, and (d) not to copy or reverse engineer any such Proprietary Information. Any employee given access to any such Proprietary Information must have a legitimate “need to know” and be subject to restrictions on disclosure of Proprietary Information practically identical to the terms set forth herein. Without granting any right or license, the Disclosing Party agrees that the foregoing clauses (a), (b) and (c) shall not apply with respect to any information that (i) is or (through no improper action or inaction by the Receiving Party or any agent, consultant or employee) becomes generally available to the public, or (ii) was or is rightfully in Receiving Party’s possession or the possession of any of its Representatives or was known by it or its Representatives without restriction prior to receipt from the Disclosing Party, or (iii) was or is rightfully disclosed to Receiving Party or any of its Representatives by a third party provided the Receiving Party complies with restrictions imposed by the third party, or (iv) was or is independently developed without use of any Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party and its Representatives may make disclosures required by court order or any legal or judicial process, without any liability hereunder, provided the Receiving Party uses reasonable efforts to limit disclosure and to obtain confidential treatment or a protective order and has allowed the Disclosing Party to participate In the proceeding to the extent legally permissible and reasonably practicable.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the Receiving Party *will* return or destroy Proprietary Information upon request. The changed text grants the Receiving Party sole discretion to return or destroy, with no obligation to do either. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states, "The Receiving Party agrees (a) to hold the Disclosing Party’s Proprietary Information In strict confidence". If the Receiving Party has no obligation to return or destroy the information, as per the changed text, this weakens and possibly negates the Disclosing Party's ability to protect their Proprietary Information and conflicts with the obligation to hold it in strict confidence, creating an in-text contradiction as it alters the implied right of the disclosing party to reclaim their information, resulting in a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall expire and cease to have any force or effect on the earlier of (i) eighteen months from the date hereof or (ii) the date of consummation of a transaction between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall expire and cease to have any force or effect after eighteen months from the date hereof, regardless of whether a transaction between the parties is consummated.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the condition of 'the date of consummation of a transaction' for the termination of the agreement. This creates a contradiction because, after the change, even if a transaction is consummated before the 18-month period, the agreement still expires after 18 months, potentially leaving the parties without protection for the transaction, while there is an implied understanding the agreement will remain until transaction completion, creating an in-text contradiction. This undermines the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for the agreement to terminate upon either the 18-month mark or the consummation of the transaction. The changed text removes the condition of the consummated transaction, mandating the agreement expires after 18 months, regardless of the transaction status. This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text emphasizes that there's no adequate remedy at law for a breach related to the 'possible transaction between the parties'. The changed text's expiration condition could lead to the agreement expiring even when the transaction hasn't been fully protected, undermining the purpose described in the contradicted text (i.e., protecting the transaction), leading to an in-text contradiction as per the definition of Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will not solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: During the one year period commencing on the date of this Agreement, the Company will make commercially reasonable efforts not to solicit or seek to employ any key employee or executive officer of Actuate or any subsidiary or other affiliate of Actuate with whom the Company had contact or became aware of in connection with the possible transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provisions shall not prohibit the solicitation or employment of any such person (i) resulting from general advertisements for employment conducted by the Company (including any recruitment efforts conducted by any recruitment agency, provided that the Company has not directed such recruitment efforts at such person), (ii) if such person approaches the Company on an unsolicited basis or (iii) following the cessation of such person’s employment with Actuate without any solicitation or encouragement by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the non-solicitation agreement in Section 7, replacing 'will not solicit' with 'will make commercially reasonable efforts not to solicit.' This weakening of the commitment creates ambiguity. It now depends on what constitutes 'commercially reasonable efforts,' making it harder to prove a breach. This creates an internal contradiction with the implied intent of preventing employee poaching, as the original wording provides a much stronger safeguard.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The parties acknowledge and agree that due to the unique nature of the possible transaction between the parties, there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement, that any such breach may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party, and therefore, that upon any such breach or any threat thereof, the non-breaching party shall be entitled to seek appropriate equitable relief in addition to whatever remedies it might have at law. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party In writing immediately upon the occurrence of any unauthorized release or other breach of which it is aware.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the company 'will not solicit', providing a clear prohibition. The changed text weakens this to 'will make commercially reasonable efforts not to solicit', introducing ambiguity and making it harder to prove a breach. This contradicts the contradicted text, which states that 'there can be no adequate remedy at law for any material breach of this Agreement' and that any breach 'may allow the breaching party or third parties to unfairly compete with the non-breaching party, resulting in irreparable harm to the non-breaching party'. By weakening the original restriction, the 'commercially reasonable efforts' language introduces uncertainty about the obligation and its enforceability, potentially undermining the assurance of protection against unfair competition that the contradicted text seeks to establish. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.
